title: Returns (On Bachmann)
description: Tom McCarthy presents "Returns (on Bachmann)", a performance-lecture delivered at the Austrian Cultural Forum New York on 21 April 2026. McCarthy argues that Bachmann should be understood not only as one of the major voices of postwar European literature, but also as a vital model for artistic and social practices concerned with memory, recurrence, and unresolved histories.Drawing on philosophical, historical, and psychoanalytic conceptions of return, McCarthy examines the recur
hook: Tom McCarthy argues Bachmann isn't just major—she's essential reading for understanding how postwar Europe processed trauma through language.

title: Love and death wear familiar faces in the harrowing Australian horror Leviticus
description: Leviticus gives the creeping metaphor of It Follows a queer specificity, making its horror just as personal and affecting.
hook: Australian horror that makes queerness the center of its dread, turning a creeping metaphor into something achingly personal.

title: The Places We Love Are Disappearing. These Authors Are Grappling With It
description:
There is a particular feeling that accompanies loving something as you watch it disappear. It is not quite despair and not quite hope. It lives somewhere in between, in the place where grief and stubbornness meet. Literature feels particularly suited to capturing that emotional contradiction because novels can hold multiple truths at once. They can […]

The post The Places We Love Are Disa
hook: Writers capturing the specific grief-and-stubbornness of watching beloved places vanish—literature as resistance to disappearance.

title: John Early on Maddie’s Secret
description: The comedian and actor discusses his directorial debut, a wonderfully original send-up of TV movies, erotic thrillers, and more
hook: John Early's directorial debut deconstructs TV movies and erotic thrillers with the precision of someone who loves what he's dismantling.

title: ‘David Bowie Was a Crazy Workaholic’: Labyrinth at 40 – an Oral History
description: "Today, Jim Henson’s dark fairytale is seen as a classic of 80s high camp. But on release, it bombed."
hook: Labyrinth bombed in 1981 because nobody was ready for Henson's dark fairytale—now it's camp royalty. An oral history of timing and taste.

title: On the Other Side Is March
description: I’m a woman in my early sixties. Somewhere between late and never. No longer the career woman, mother, housewife and lover doing it all, meeting every demand, and then some, just with my left hand. Now I’m wife, mother, grandmother,
hook: A woman in her sixties stops performing perfection and discovers what's on the other side: March, and everything it means.

title: Candice Carty-Williams: ‘People feel very attached to Queenie’
description:
The breakout success of her debut created a publishing scramble for Black writers, but has that appetite for diversity endured? Carty-Williams talks about wanting to quit the TV adaptation, why now is the perfect time for her sequel

One of the questions Candice Carty-Williams has spent the past few years batting away is whether she is Queenie. It is perhaps inevitable: her best­selling debut n
hook: Candice Carty-Williams on why her breakout sequel arrives now, and whether publishing's diversity appetite survived the initial scramble.
